evidence: Top-level structure follows clear domain alignment: src/lib/data/ for static content, src/lib/utils/ for API helpers, src/lib/components/ for UI, src/lib/types/ for shared interfaces, src/routes/ for SvelteKit routing
Each data file (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) has a single, obvious responsibility with typed exports
The public surface is minimal: data files export typed arrays, utils export focused functions, types export clean interfaces
+page.server.ts orchestrates GitHub data fetching and attachment cleanly, composing fetchAllRepoData and attachGithubData
+layout.ts is minimal (2 lines: prerender and csr flags), which is appropriate for a static site
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: The Nib project in metaProjects shares a repo path with The Work (JasonWarrenUK/the-work), which means two logically distinct projects will receive the same GitHub data at runtime, a minor data model inaccuracy. The codebase is very small (8 production files, 313 LOC), so decomposition quality is somewhat trivially achieved; the structure is sound but has not yet been tested by growth.

evidence: +page.server.ts composes data flow clearly: collect repo paths -> fetchAllRepoData -> attachGithubData per category -> return structured data

The boundary between static data (src/lib/data/) and dynamic enrichment (src/lib/utils/github.ts) is clean: data files export Omit<Project, 'github'>[], and attachGithubData produces full Project[] by merging GitHub data
fetchGitHub and tryFetchJson form a clear two-layer internal API in github.ts: raw fetch with error handling, then JSON parsing layer on top
Error propagation at the GitHub API boundary is consistent: failures return null, callers handle null gracefully with fallback defaults (empty languages, 0 contributors)
Data translation happens in exactly one place: attachGithubData maps from the lookup dictionary to project objects
mid_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: The handoff between +page.server.ts and +page.svelte uses three separate properties (impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects) rather than a single structured object, which is a minor verbosity concern but follows SvelteKit conventions naturally.

evidence: github.ts has thorough unit tests covering all exported functions: parseCommitCount (5 cases), isRepoResponse (4 cases), isLanguagesResponse (5 cases), fetchRepoData (6 cases including error paths), fetchAllRepoData (2 cases)
Data files (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) all have structural validation tests confirming data shape and invariants
page.server.test.ts tests attachGithubData with 5 cases covering match, miss, explicit null, mixed data, and field preservation
No snapshot tests; all tests are assertion-based with clear expectations
Tests mock fetch at the global level using vi.stubGlobal, which is appropriate for this context but means no integration test hits the real GitHub API
Svelte components (9 files in src/lib/components/) have no test coverage at all

github.ts exports a coherent API surface: three type guards (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse, parseCommitCount), two async data fetchers (fetchRepoData, fetchAllRepoData), and one sync transformer (attachGithubData)
The type guards are sync and the data fetchers are async, which is a natural split by purpose rather than an inconsistency

All async functions return Promise-wrapped types consistently: fetchGitHub returns Promise<Response|null>, tryFetchJson returns Promise<unknown>, fetchRepoData returns Promise<ProjectGitHubData|null>, fetchAllRepoData returns Promise<Record<string, ProjectGitHubData|null>>
Data modules (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) all export named const arrays with consistent patterns
parseCommitCount mixes sync/async in the same module as fetchRepoData, but this is flagged in scan_evidence; the function is a pure parser logically distinct from the async fetchers
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: The sync/async mix in github.ts is intentional (type guards and parsers vs. fetchers) but the module's public API surface blends these without explicit grouping or documentation of the different categories. The tryFetchJson return type of Promise<unknown> is accurate but forces callers to do their own type narrowing.

evidence: Directory profiles show clean separation: src/lib/data/ (3 files, 144 LOC, avg fan-in 1.0), src/routes/ (2 files, 21 LOC, avg fan-in 0.0)
src/lib/ follows a clear four-directory taxonomy: components/ (9 files), data/ (3 files), types/ (1 file), utils/ (1 file)
No flat directory overload: the largest directory (components/) has 9 files, all Svelte components with consistent naming
No straggler root files outside framework-expected locations: app.css, app.html, and app.d.ts are standard SvelteKit root files
constants.ts sits at src/lib/ root level with fan-in of 1 (imported only by types/index.test.ts and potentially components); this is borderline but acceptable for a single constants file
Coupling matrix shows no bidirectional or problematic cross-directory edges; data/ has fan-out of 0 (pure data, no imports beyond types), utils/ imports from types only
Total codebase is 8 production files / 313 LOC, well under the <20 file threshold where flat structure would be appropriate, yet the project maintains useful subdirectory organization
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: The types/ directory contains a single index.ts file, which is fine for now but could become a concern if more type files are added without a clear organization strategy. The constants.ts file at src/lib/ root is a minor placement question: it could live in data/ or types/ given its tight coupling to the SectionAccent type.

evidence: All data modules (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) export pure constant arrays with no side effects
github.ts defines API_BASE and GITHUB_HEADERS as simple string/object literals at module scope with no dynamic computation or environment reads
+page.server.ts performs network calls only inside the async load() function, not at import time
+layout.ts exports two boolean constants (prerender, csr) with no side effects
No circular dependencies detected in the import graph
No environment variable reads at module scope anywhere in the codebase
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: API_BASE and GITHUB_HEADERS are module-level constants; while harmless for this codebase size, they represent a minor rigidity if the API base ever needed to be configurable per-environment

evidence: github.ts has a clean layered decomposition: fetchGitHub (transport) -> tryFetchJson (parse) -> fetchRepoData (single repo) -> fetchAllRepoData (batch) -> attachGithubData (merge)
+page.server.ts is a focused 19-line orchestration that collects repos, fetches data, and returns enriched projects
Data files (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) are pure typed constant exports with no mixed concerns
+page.svelte uses data-driven sectionTints record for background color transitions rather than imperative branching
+page.svelte hardcodes a repo string check to conditionally render Terminal for the Iris project, mixing presentation routing with data
design_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: The hardcoded repo-path check in +page.svelte for Terminal rendering is a minor design concern where presentation logic is coupled to a specific data value rather than being driven by the data model

evidence: Function names in github.ts follow a consistent vocabulary: fetch* for network operations, is* for type guards, parse* for data extraction, attach* for enrichment
Variable names like repoRaw, langRaw, contribRaw use a 'Raw' suffix to clearly signal unvalidated data before type narrowing
Data module names (impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects) are domain-aligned and descriptive
Type names (ProjectGitHubData, TerminalFrame, SectionAccent) precisely describe their purpose
Constant names API_BASE and GITHUB_HEADERS follow SCREAMING_CASE convention for module-level constants
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: The function tryFetchJson uses a 'try' prefix that in some conventions implies boolean success/failure, though in this codebase it consistently means 'attempt and return null on failure', which is a valid convention

evidence: fetchGitHub uses a clean guard clause (early return on !res.ok) followed by a try/catch for network errors, with no redundant branches
tryFetchJson chains fetchGitHub with a null check and a separate try/catch for JSON parsing, each failure path returns null consistently
Type guard functions (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) use progressive narrowing: typeof check, null check, then field-level validation
parseCommitCount has a clear three-way logic: regex match on Link header, fallback to 1 if response existed, 0 if it did not
The reveal $effect in +page.svelte correctly returns early for prefers-reduced-motion, and both $effects return cleanup functions
No dead code paths, no always-true/false conditions, no async functions that never await
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: The parseCommitCount parameter name 'responseOk' slightly misrepresents its semantics (it signals response existence rather than HTTP status), but the logic itself is correct and consistent

evidence: All public functions in github.ts have explicit return type annotations that accurately cover all code paths
Type guard functions (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) use proper 'data is T' predicates with runtime validation matching the declared shape
Project data files consistently use Omit<Project, 'github'>[] to express that static data lacks the runtime-enriched github field
attachGithubData correctly narrows Omit<Project, 'github'>[] to Project[] through the spread-and-assign pattern
RepoResponse interface captures only the fields actually used from the GitHub API, avoiding over-typing
tryFetchJson returns Promise<unknown> which is technically correct but does not distinguish the null failure case from a valid parsed result
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: tryFetchJson's return type of Promise<unknown> is honest but imprecise; callers must independently handle the null case that is hidden within 'unknown'. A return type of Promise<unknown | null> would be semantically redundant in TS but a type like Promise<JsonValue | null> with a defined JsonValue type would better communicate the contract.

evidence: tryFetchJson declares return type Promise<unknown> but silently returns null on both fetch failure and JSON parse failure, making it unclear to callers whether null means 'request failed' or 'parse failed'
fetchRepoData returns Promise<ProjectGitHubData | null> where null conflates 'API unreachable' with 'response did not match expected shape'
parseCommitCount accepts responseOk: boolean but the caller passes (commitsRes !== null) which is a nullity check, not a response.ok check; the parameter name implies HTTP status semantics

attachGithubData signature says it returns Project[] but input type is Omit<Project, 'github'>[], so it transforms partial types into full types; the function name 'attach' correctly implies augmentation
Data module exports (artefacts, impactProjects, etc.) all honor their declared types cleanly with no contract violations
contract_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: parseCommitCount parameter name misleads about expected semantics; null return from tryFetchJson and fetchRepoData conflates distinct failure modes without distinguishing them

evidence: github.ts uses a consistent return-null strategy for all error paths: fetchGitHub returns null on non-ok or catch, tryFetchJson returns null on null response or JSON parse failure, fetchRepoData returns null when type guards fail
console.warn is used consistently with [GitHub API] tags for all error logging in github.ts
The catch block in tryFetchJson line 26 swallows the error entirely — no logging, no context preservation, just returns null
Error context is partially lost: fetchGitHub preserves the error message in its log, but tryFetchJson's catch does not log which parse error occurred
No error handling exists at the +page.server.ts level — if fetchAllRepoData throws an unexpected error (e.g. from Promise.all rejection), it will surface as an unhandled SSG build failure
No pass-through wrappers detected; every function adds meaningful behavior (type validation, data transformation, error handling)
Type interfaces (Project, Artefact, TerminalFrame, ProjectGitHubData) are lean and directly used by their consumers with no unnecessary indirection
attachGithubData performs a genuine transformation (Omit<Project, 'github'>[] to Project[]) rather than just forwarding data
fetchGitHub wraps fetch with error handling and header injection — genuine added behavior, not ceremony
tryFetchJson builds on fetchGitHub adding JSON parsing — each layer adds distinct value
No single-implementation interfaces; the interfaces defined in types/index.ts are used across multiple files
The codebase is flat and direct: data files export typed arrays, utils export functions, routes consume them
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: The three project arrays (impactProjects, explorationProjects, metaProjects) in projects.ts share the same type and are consumed identically in +page.server.ts, creating mild repetition in the data pipeline that could be simplified with a single categorized collection

evidence: Comment ratio is extremely low (0.003) — the codebase is almost entirely uncommented, which is the opposite of AI-generated bloat
Three comments in github.ts are '// Exported for testing' which are functional annotations, not restating comments
No defensive overengineering detected: null checks in github.ts are all at genuine I/O boundaries (network fetch, JSON parse, type validation)
No docstring bloat: zero JSDoc or multi-line documentation blocks on any functions
No generic naming patterns (handleData, processItem) — function names are domain-specific (fetchRepoData, attachGithubData, parseCommitCount)
No boilerplate error handling duplication — the two try-catch blocks in github.ts handle genuinely different failure modes (network vs parse)
Terminal.svelte comment '// Match . or ." as sentence boundaries' in splitOverview is in ProjectCard.svelte and explains a non-obvious regex, adding genuine value
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: The '// Exported for testing' comments appear three times on consecutive functions in github.ts; this pattern could be expressed once or replaced with a structural convention (e.g. grouping exports)

evidence: fetchRepoData assembles four parallel API calls, validates each with appropriate type guards, and returns a clean data object — direct control flow with no unnecessary nesting
splitOverview in ProjectCard.svelte uses a single regex match with clear boundary handling — concise and precise
getLanguageSegments chains sort/slice/map in a natural pipeline with a well-placed guard clause for zero total
Terminal.svelte playFrames is a clean async loop with per-frame delay — minimal complexity for the animation behavior
The two $effect blocks in +page.svelte each handle distinct concerns (reveal animation, background tinting) with clean observer setup and teardown

low_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: The two $effect blocks in +page.svelte follow similar patterns (create observer, query elements, observe, return cleanup) but are not extracted to a shared helper despite structural similarity; Section.svelte packs multiple conditional style computations into a single inline attribute

evidence: Import graph is shallow and acyclic: routes/+page.server.ts imports from lib/data/projects.ts and lib/utils/github.ts; routes/+page.svelte imports from lib/data/artefacts.ts, lib/data/terminal.ts, and lib/components/
No god modules detected: the most-imported files each have only 1 importer
Clear unidirectional dependency flow: routes -> lib/utils and lib/data -> lib/types
No shared mutable state or import-time side effects across modules
Total codebase is 313 LOC across 8 production files, limiting the surface area for architectural issues
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: The codebase is small enough that architectural patterns are trivially correct rather than demonstrably maintained under pressure. The lib/types/index.ts barrel file is the sole type hub for the entire codebase, which is fine at this scale but would become a coupling point as the project grows.

evidence: All data modules in src/lib/data/ follow the same pattern: import type from '$lib/types', export named const array with explicit type annotation

Type definitions in lib/types/index.ts use consistent interface declarations with no mixed strategies
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: Minor inconsistency in GitHub casing: the type is 'ProjectGitHubData' (capital H) but the function is 'attachGithubData' (lowercase h). This is a trivial cosmetic drift but technically a naming convention inconsistency.

evidence: package.json has zero runtime (non-dev) dependencies; every dependency is a devDependency

dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Version ranges use caret (^) notation which allows minor version drift; this is standard practice and not a real concern for a static site, but it is technically less pinned than exact versions.

evidence: This is a static portfolio site with no authentication, no user accounts, no protected resources, and no server-side routes requiring authorization
The only server-side code is +page.server.ts which fetches public GitHub API data at build time; there are no runtime API endpoints
All routes are public by design: a single-page portfolio with prerender = true and adapter-static
The GitHub API calls in github.ts use no authentication tokens; they access only public repository data
The holistic_context.authorization object is empty, confirming no auth patterns exist in the codebase
All source files are TypeScript (.ts or .svelte); no mixed JS/TS files indicating incomplete TypeScript migration
No TODO, FIXME, DEPRECATED, 'legacy', 'migrate', 'old api', or 'remove after' markers found anywhere in the src/ directory
No deprecated-but-called symbols or compatibility shims exist in the codebase
The codebase uses Svelte 5 runes ($props, $state, $effect) uniformly; no Svelte 4 patterns (export let, $:, onMount for reactive declarations) coexist
The holistic_context.migration_signals object is empty, confirming no migration residue was detected by mechanical scans
Uses modern SvelteKit patterns throughout: Snippet-based children, interface Props pattern, $props() destructuring

strengths: Directory structure maps cleanly to domain concerns: data, components, types, utils, and routes each have coherent ownership
Public exports are minimal and consistently typed; every data file imports its type from $lib/types and exports a single named array
The single-page architecture decision is honestly reflected in the file layout: one +page.svelte composes all sections, one +page.server.ts handles all data fetching
No surprising cross-cutting files or misplaced concerns; a new engineer could navigate this immediately
high_level_elegance.issue_character: No structural decomposition issues; the one minor concern is a data modeling edge case where two logical projects share a repository identifier.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates clear, domain-aligned decomposition with predictable ownership boundaries. A developer can explain why every file exists and where to find or add things. The score sits at 88 rather than higher because the codebase is small enough that good structure is relatively easy to achieve, and there is a minor data model ambiguity with the shared repo path. Against the global anchors, this is solidly in the 'strong' range where a developer would trust what they see.

strengths: The data enrichment pipeline (static data -> GitHub API -> merged output) is a clean composition pattern with one translation point in attachGithubData

Error boundaries at the GitHub API layer are consistent: fetchGitHub returns Response|null, tryFetchJson returns unknown (with null fallback), and fetchRepoData returns ProjectGitHubData|null, propagating gracefully
The Omit<Project, 'github'> type on data exports makes the handoff contract explicit: data files provide everything except GitHub data, and the server load function adds it
No glue-code entropy: there are no ad-hoc mappers or boundary conditionals between modules
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: No material handoff or integration seam issues were found; the module boundaries are well-defined with explicit contracts.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates clean, explicit handoffs across all module boundaries. The data enrichment pipeline from static exports through GitHub API to page data follows a clear composition pattern. Error propagation is consistent and predictable. This rates as 'strong' (90) because a developer would trust the seam design and encounter no surprising implicit contracts. The small codebase size means fewer seams to evaluate, but the ones that exist are well-crafted.

strengths: github.ts has comprehensive unit tests covering success paths, error paths (network error, non-ok response, malformed JSON, non-Error thrown values), and edge cases (deduplication, partial failures)
Type guard functions (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) are independently tested with positive and negative cases
Data integrity tests validate structural invariants (non-empty arrays, required fields, owner/repo format, intentional duplicate handling for Nib/The Work)
Test helpers (jsonResponse, errorResponse) are well-structured and reusable within the test file
test_strategy.issue_character: The untested area is concentrated in the Svelte component layer, which is common for small projects but represents the largest subsystem by file count.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The TypeScript logic layer has strong test coverage with thoughtful error-path testing. The data validation tests add confidence that content changes will not silently break invariants. However, nine Svelte components representing the entire presentation layer have zero test coverage. For a static portfolio site, this is a reasonable pragmatic trade-off, but it means a developer modifying components has no safety net. This places the score at 82: solid coverage where it exists, but a meaningful gap in the component layer keeps it from being 'strong' overall.

strengths: All async data-fetching functions follow a consistent null-return contract for failures, making error behavior predictable
Function parameter shapes are consistent: repo identifiers are always string paths in owner/repo format
Data module exports are uniformly shaped: named const arrays with explicit element types
attachGithubData has a clean, predictable signature: takes partial projects and a lookup dictionary, returns complete projects
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The API surface is coherent overall; the one minor concern is the mixed sync/async exports in github.ts, which is a natural consequence of co-locating related utilities rather than a design flaw.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: A developer can predict API shapes and behavior from seeing one example in this codebase. The github.ts module's public surface mixes sync helpers with async fetchers, which is a reasonable design choice but not immediately obvious to a new consumer. The data modules are exemplary in their consistency. This rates at 85: solidly in the 'strong' range with a minor friction point that does not materially impede usage.

strengths: Directory names clearly communicate contained responsibilities: data/ for static content, components/ for UI, types/ for interfaces, utils/ for helper functions
No import-affinity mismatches: files are placed in directories matching their primary concern and import sources

package_organization.issue_character: No structural organization issues; the directory layout is clean and navigable with no misplaced files or ambiguous boundaries.
package_organization.score_rationale: A developer can predict where to find and put things based on directory structure alone. The four-directory taxonomy under src/lib/ is clear and consistently applied. The codebase is small (8 production files) but organized as if it were larger, which is forward-looking. The score of 89 reflects a 'strong' layout with only trivial observations (single-file types/ directory, root-level constants.ts) that do not impede navigation or ownership clarity.

strengths: All network calls are deferred to the SvelteKit load() function, never executed at import time

The only reason this is not 100 is the hardcoded API_BASE constant, which is a very minor rigidity rather than a true coupling defect. Per global anchors, this is solidly in the 90+ range.

strengths: github.ts demonstrates clean functional layering: each function has a single focused responsibility and composes naturally
Data modules are pure declarations with zero behavioral code, cleanly separating data from logic

strengths: Type guard functions (isRepoResponse, isLanguagesResponse) correctly narrow unknown to specific types and return accurate boolean contracts
attachGithubData accurately transforms Omit<Project, 'github'>[] into Project[] with correct nullish coalescing
Data modules (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) export exactly what their types promise with no hidden behavior
PageServerLoad function in +page.server.ts returns a clear object matching expected SvelteKit conventions
contract_coherence.issue_character: Issues are isolated to the github.ts utility module where null-returning functions conflate distinct failure modes and one parameter name misleads about its semantic meaning.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The data layer and route modules honor their contracts cleanly. The github.ts module has minor contract ambiguities: null return values conflate different failure scenarios, and parseCommitCount's responseOk parameter name implies HTTP status checking when it actually receives a nullity check. These are localized issues that would cause minor confusion when debugging API failures but do not create systemic trust problems. This places the codebase solidly above 80 (uneven) but below 90 (strong).

strengths: github.ts applies a consistent return-null graceful degradation pattern across all its functions
All console.warn calls in github.ts use a consistent [GitHub API] tag prefix for log filtering
fetchGitHub correctly extracts error messages from caught exceptions using instanceof Error check
The overall design intent is clear: GitHub data is supplementary, so failures degrade gracefully to null rather than crashing the build
error_consistency.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in github.ts where the otherwise consistent null-return strategy has gaps: one catch block silently swallows without logging, and the page server has no protection against unexpected Promise rejections during build.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a clear error philosophy (graceful degradation via null returns) that is mostly applied consistently. However, the tryFetchJson catch block silently swallows JSON parse errors without any logging, breaking the otherwise consistent pattern of warning on failures. Additionally, +page.server.ts has no try-catch around the GitHub data fetching, meaning unexpected errors (not covered by the internal null-return pattern) would crash the SSG build. These gaps pull the score below 80, as a developer working here would encounter inconsistency in how thoroughly errors are logged and handled at boundaries.

Functions are purposefully named, comments are rare and meaningful, there is no defensive overengineering, and no boilerplate duplication. The repeated '// Exported for testing' annotation is the only formulaic pattern, and it is functional rather than noise. This places the codebase at approximately 90: a developer's attention is spent on logic, not filtering noise, with only trivial friction.

strengths: fetchRepoData uses Promise.all for parallel fetching then validates each result with dedicated type guards — control flow is direct and each step is distinct
Guard clauses are used consistently: getLanguageSegments returns early for zero total, parseCommitCount handles the linkHeader path first
playFrames in Terminal.svelte is a clean async for-of loop with minimal nesting — the delay/accumulate pattern is immediately readable
splitOverview is a well-proportioned helper: 4 lines of logic for a non-trivial text splitting task, no over-extraction
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are minor and localized: slight duplication in observer setup patterns in +page.svelte and a dense inline style expression in Section.svelte.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Function and component internals throughout the codebase are concise, direct, and proportionate. Control flow uses guard clauses, pipelines, and clean async patterns without unnecessary nesting or defensive sprawl. The two observer $effect blocks in +page.svelte share a structural pattern that could be extracted, and Section.svelte packs conditional styling into a dense inline attribute. These are minor rough edges in otherwise clean local implementation. This places the codebase above 85 (solid craft) but below 90 (isolated rough edges only).

strengths: Dependency direction is strictly unidirectional: routes depend on lib, lib/data depends on lib/types, lib/utils depends on lib/types; no reverse imports
No cycles exist in the import graph; each module has a clear, singular responsibility
No shared mutable state or import-time side effects; all data modules export pure constants, and github.ts exports pure async functions
The boundary between build-time data fetching (+page.server.ts) and client-side rendering (+page.svelte) is cleanly separated through SvelteKit conventions
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: No material cross-module architecture issues exist; the codebase is small, cleanly layered, and free of boundary violations.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The dependency graph is acyclic, boundaries are respected, and no hub modules or shared mutable state patterns exist. This earns a strong score. The score is held slightly below exemplary because the codebase is only 313 LOC across 8 files, meaning the architecture has not yet been tested under growth pressure; the current simplicity is more a function of scale than of deliberate architectural discipline under complexity.

strengths: All three data modules (artefacts.ts, projects.ts, terminal.ts) follow an identical structural pattern: typed import, named const export of a typed array
Consistent camelCase naming across all functions in utils/github.ts with clear verb prefixes (fetch, is, parse, attach)
Svelte components uniformly use PascalCase file naming and Svelte 5 runes ($props, $effect) throughout
Export patterns are uniform: all modules use named exports exclusively, no default exports
Type strategy is consistent: all domain objects use TypeScript interfaces, no mixed TypedDict/class/NamedTuple patterns
convention_outlier.issue_character: The only convention inconsistency found is a trivial casing variation in a compound word (GitHub vs Github), which is cosmetic and does not impede navigation or understanding.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: A developer can confidently predict conventions from seeing one module and trust they hold elsewhere. The data/ directory is a model of sibling consistency. The single minor casing inconsistency (GitHub vs Github) prevents a perfect score but does not materially affect developer experience. This places the codebase solidly in the 90+ range per the dimension anchors.

strengths: Zero runtime production dependencies; the entire dependency set is build-time tooling
Uses native fetch API in github.ts rather than pulling in an HTTP client library
No utility libraries (lodash, ramda, etc.) for trivial operations; all logic is hand-written
Each devDependency serves a clear, non-overlapping purpose in the build pipeline: framework (SvelteKit/Svelte), styling (Tailwind), language (TypeScript), bundler (Vite), test runner (Vitest)
Lock file (bun.lock) is committed, ensuring reproducible installs
dependency_health.issue_character: No dependency health issues were found; the dependency set is minimal, purposeful, and free of redundancy.
dependency_health.score_rationale: This is a near-exemplary dependency setup. Zero runtime dependencies for a static site is the ideal state. Every devDependency is essential and non-overlapping. No duplicate libraries, no heavy deps for light use, no circular imports. The score is set at 95 rather than 100 because the codebase is too small for dependency health to be meaningfully tested under pressure, but what exists is clean and intentional.

strengths: Authorization is not applicable to this codebase; it is a statically generated portfolio site with no user-facing authentication or protected resources
The build-time GitHub API access correctly uses unauthenticated public endpoints, which is appropriate for public repo data
authorization_consistency.issue_character: No authorization-related issues exist because the codebase has no authorization concerns by design.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This dimension is not applicable to a static portfolio site. There are no protected resources, no user sessions, no API endpoints requiring auth, and no server-side route handlers. The absence of auth is intentional and correct. Scoring 100 because there is nothing to get wrong here, and nothing has been gotten wrong.

strengths: Complete TypeScript adoption with zero .js source files
Uniform Svelte 5 runes usage across all components; no Svelte 4 reactivity patterns coexist
No deprecated markers, legacy TODOs, or migration shims exist anywhere in the source
Modern SvelteKit conventions used consistently: Snippet children pattern in +layout.svelte, interface Props with $props()
incomplete_migration.issue_character: No incomplete migration issues exist; the codebase shows no evidence of API coexistence, deprecated symbols, or stale migration artifacts.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase is fully migrated to its current stack: all TypeScript, all Svelte 5 runes, all modern SvelteKit patterns. No old/new pattern coexistence was found. No deprecated symbols or compatibility shims exist. This is an exemplary score because migrations are complete and there is no legacy residue to confuse a developer about which pattern to follow.

issues.summary: Nine Svelte components have zero test coverage
issues.suggestion: Prioritize testing components with logic beyond pure rendering: Terminal.svelte (animation timing), ProjectCard.svelte (conditional terminal slot, accent handling), and Artefacts.svelte (data display). Use @testing-library/svelte for component tests.
evidence: src/lib/components/ contains 9 .svelte files, none with corresponding test files
These components handle rendering logic, intersection observer setup, animation, and conditional display, which are non-trivial behaviors

issues.summary: Template logic hardcodes a repo path string to conditionally render Terminal component
issues.suggestion: Add an optional 'terminal' or 'showcase' field to the Project type or to the project data entries, so the template can check a boolean/object property instead of matching against a hardcoded repo path string
evidence: Line 98: project.repo === 'foundersandcoders/iris' is a magic string check that couples the template to a specific project's identity rather than a data-driven property

issues.summary: tryFetchJson returns Promise<unknown> without explicitly representing the null failure case
issues.suggestion: Change the return type to Promise<unknown | null> or define a type alias. While unknown technically includes null in TypeScript, the explicit union communicates the contract: this function can fail and will return null when it does.
evidence: Lines 21-30: tryFetchJson can return null on two failure paths (fetchGitHub returns null, or JSON parse fails) but the return type Promise<unknown> subsumes null without signaling it. Callers like fetchRepoData (line 74) must independently know to check for null via the isRepoResponse guard.

issues.summary: parseCommitCount's responseOk parameter name implies HTTP ok status but receives a nullity check
issues.suggestion: Rename parameter from 'responseOk' to 'hasResponse' or 'responseReceived' to accurately reflect that callers pass a nullity check, not an HTTP status check
evidence: parseCommitCount(commitsRes?.headers.get('Link') ?? null, commitsRes !== null) — the second argument is a null check, but the parameter name 'responseOk' suggests res.ok semantics

issues.summary: fetchRepoData returns null for both network failures and schema mismatches without distinction
issues.suggestion: Consider returning a discriminated result type or logging different warning messages at the fetchRepoData level to distinguish API shape changes from network failures, even if the return type stays nullable for graceful degradation
evidence: fetchRepoData returns null both when fetchGitHub fails (network error) and when isRepoResponse returns false (unexpected API shape); callers cannot distinguish 'GitHub is down' from 'API response changed'

issues.summary: tryFetchJson catch block swallows JSON parse errors without logging, unlike sibling error handlers
issues.suggestion: Add error context to the catch block: catch (e) { console.warn('[GitHub API] JSON parse failed', { url, error: e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e) }); return null; }
evidence: Line 26-28: catch block returns null without any console.warn, while fetchGitHub (lines 14-17) logs errors with context; the console.warn on line 27 only logs a bare message without the parse error details

issues.summary: +page.server.ts has no error boundary around GitHub API calls that could fail during SSG build
issues.suggestion: Wrap the load function body in a try-catch that returns empty GitHub data on failure, ensuring the static build always succeeds even if the GitHub API is completely unreachable or returns unexpected errors
evidence: The load function directly awaits fetchAllRepoData with no try-catch; while fetchRepoData handles individual failures gracefully, Promise.all in fetchAllRepoData could reject if an unexpected error occurs outside the try-catch in fetchGitHub

issues.summary: Two $effect blocks in +page.svelte follow identical observer-setup-cleanup patterns without extraction
issues.suggestion: Extract a reusable createSectionObserver helper that accepts observer options and a callback, reducing each $effect to a single function call with distinct configuration
evidence: Lines 31-52 and 54-75 both create IntersectionObserver, query DOM elements, observe them, and return cleanup functions — the structure is parallel even though the callbacks differ

issues.summary: Inconsistent casing of 'GitHub' in type name vs function name
issues.suggestion: Rename 'attachGithubData' to 'attachGitHubData' in utils/github.ts and update the call site in routes/+page.server.ts to match
evidence: Type is named 'ProjectGitHubData' (capital H) in types/index.ts, but the function is named 'attachGithubData' (lowercase h) in utils/github.ts
